summary.category: Classification and Definition of Economic Sectors
points: Economic activities are classified into three interconnected sectors: primary (extraction of raw materials from nature), secondary (processing and manufacturing raw materials into finished goods), and tertiary (provision of services supporting both sectors). Each sector creates monetary value and together they form an integrated system where primary provides raw materials, secondary adds value through transformation, and tertiary enables distribution and supports all economic functions.

summary.category: Characteristics and Examples of Each Sector
points: Primary sector includes agriculture, mining, fishing, forestry, and livestock rearing—activities directly dependent on nature. Secondary sector encompasses manufacturing, construction, and processing activities that transform raw materials into finished products with greater value. Tertiary sector covers transportation, banking, communication, healthcare, education, and retail services that enable commerce and meet consumer needs.

The three sectors are fundamentally interdependent—primary outputs become secondary inputs, secondary products require tertiary distribution, and all sectors depend on tertiary services to function. The AMUL cooperative demonstrates this interdependence perfectly: farmers (primary) produce milk, cooperative factories (secondary) process it into value-added products, and transportation and retail networks (tertiary) deliver to consumers across India and internationally. This cooperative model shows how organized collective action eliminates exploitative middlemen and creates shared prosperity while illustrating how modern economies convert natural resources into goods and services meeting consumer needs.
